data.title: Limoneira Aiming For Growth, But Results Are Likely Years Off
data.description:
Lemons hold a special place in culinary culture. I like to cook a lot of seafood, and cooking it in lemon juice is for many things almost obligatory, at least so far as I’m concerned. But we also just take lemons for granted, and never really think about how supplying them fits into the economy.

That’s where Limoneira (LMNR), our topic for today, comes in. One of the largest lemon growers in North America, and a couple big stories came out about them this week, so let’s give the humble lemon it’s day in the sun.

We’ll be talking both about their recent Q2 earnings release, which was a miss on revenue and EPS followed by lower guidance for production, and the news that they’re returning to a citrus partnership with Sunkist which they left back in 2010. We’ll be talking about both of those, as well as their aim to grow their avocado productions.

Limoneira: More Than Just Lemons

Wait, avocados? Weren’t we just talking about lemons? Yes we were, but Limoneira is more than just a lemon grower, though that’s their claim to fame. They also produce other citrus products and yes, grow a pretty large crop of avocados.

Limoneira has a surprisingly diverse business for a lemon farmer, because not only is it not just lemons, it’s not just farming either. Limoneira has a lot of land rights and water rights, and some of that’s in relatively high-value parts of California, so they have some real estate deals where they can flip the farmland for quite a bit more than its listed as being worth on the balance sheet.

Cash

$2.1 million

Total Current Assets

$29.8 million

Total Current Liabilities

$28.5 million

Long-Term Debt

$54.9 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$182.7 million

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

The current ratio is serviceable for an agribusiness, but nothing to write home about. The two things I want to talk about though are the bottom two numbers. Long-term debt isn’t huge for a company their size, but it has been growing. At the end of the fiscal year, they had $40 million in long-term debt, now it’s up to $54.9 million. That could be concerning.

On a happier note though, that total shareholder equity, which is largely from property and plants, is likely undervalued compared to what Limoneira could get on the real estate market if and when the developers come calling. It means their achievable price/book ratio is realistically even lower than it already is.

The price/book ratio at current prices is 1.64, which is already quite a bit lower than the sector median of 2.73. Assuming they can keep the debt from continuing to grow, this could be a first sign that Limoneira is a bit of a contrarian play.

The Struggles of 2025

But as I mentioned, Limoneira missed in Q2 earnings. After beating EPS estimates four of the last five quarters before that, this is a disappointing surprise. 2025 is shaping up to be a tough year for them.

You can see from here where Limoneira has been headed in the wrong direction, not just in Q2, but struggling for the past year and a half. They’re not really profitable right now, and that’s going to continue through this year if estimates to be believed, with non-GAAP EPS estimates of a 24¢ gain in Q3 and a 9¢ loss in Q4.

Revenue from their lemon business was down year over year in Q2, and where they’d previously offered a guidance of 5-5.5 million cartons this year, and now it’s down to 4.5-5 million cartons.

Avocados saw decreased production in Q2 year over year too, down from 1.6 million pounds in 2024 to 1.2 million pounds in 2025. That’s not a shock though, it’s reflective of biennial bearing of avocado trees, where they have a big crop one year and a small crop the next.

It came at a bad time though, because in 2024, they were only getting paid $1.47 per pound, and in 2025 they’ve been getting paid $2.26. The higher prices are a result of, among other things, the tariffs making imported avocados from Mexico more expensive. It was a particularly big deal around the Super Bowl, when avocado consumption in the US is highest.

Unfortunately, while the highest prices this year helped Limoneira increase its avocado revenue despite lower production, it happened in one of their off years for growing, and there’s no guarantee prices will remain high next year when their trees are producing more. Though tariffs probably will still be a thing, there is also the possibility that persistent higher prices will force people toward different snacks, ultimately lowering demand and normalizing prices.

Persistent Production Challenge: Growth Takes A While

While 2025 is likely to be a challenging year, it’s not as though Limoneira can expect a snap back year in 2026, either. More of their avocado trees will be in the high-yield year, so that segment should do better assuming prices are still strong, but their guidance for 2026 lemon production is even lower than the reduced guidance for 2025, down to 4-4.5 million cartons according to the conference call.

They’re also returning to a partnership in sales and marketing with Sunkist for citrus, which we’ll talk about later, and Limoneira says they expect it to improve margins, but reduce top-line revenue. If the avocado sector doesn’t strongly make up for this reduced production, 2026 might not be a lot better, and the EPS estimate for 2026 is still only 32¢, which is still nothing exciting at Limoneira’s current stock price. Even the ambitious 2027 estimate of 71¢ per share is just okay, and that undercuts the idea it would be a good contrarian pick, as I first thought.

Growth is the key here, but there’s an obvious problem: growth doesn’t just grow on trees, or rather the issue is it does just grow on trees. Limoneira is looking to grow their avocado production substantially, but per the conference call those trees aren’t going to be mature enough until the end of 2027. That means we won’t be seeing the results of those efforts for years.

The Return to Sunkist

Then we come to Sunkist. Limoneira had a long, long history of working with Sunkist in the citrus business, but in 2010 they ended that partnership, saying they would market their own lemons, and with multiple lemon brands they owned outright, they were highly confident.

Limoneira didn’t fall flat on their face with that, but here we are, 15 years later, and they announced earlier this week that they were re-merging their citrus sale and marketing operations with Sunkist. Though we don’t have all the details of this deal, it has the appearance of a quo ante bellum deal – that is they’re going right back to where they were in 2009.

In the conference call they conceded that top-line revenue would be down because of the merger, but predicted saving some $5 million in cost savings. It’s not clear what that would translate to on the bottom line, given the lower revenue.

While they insist in the call that this merger allows them to meet “several key objectives” and insisted “we’re not changing direction,” it certainly gives the appearance that they very much are changing direction.

We’ll have to wait to see what happens here, but I believe it’s fairly obviously them rethinking their decision to try to go it alone on lemons after trying it in 2010. They also told a story about Oxnard Lemon in the conference call which tried to justify the move, but I think raises more questions than it answers.

Limoneira acquired Oxnard Lemon, a full-line lemon packer, in 2018. But then they divested the wash and storage capacity, something they described themselves as being “very fortunate” to be able to do. After that, they ended up having to lease back that capacity at a much higher than expected price. It frankly doesn’t sound fortunate to me the way they tell it.

The argument they’re making is that with access to Sunkist’s infrastructure they won’t have that kind of problem in the future, but it feels like this was largely a self-created problem that they’re trying to solve by reversing another of the actions they had second thoughts about, splitting up their partnership with Sunkist. It makes me question the decision-making that underpins all of this.

No Rush to Buy Here

In the end, Limoneira does have some value locked away here, primarily in their real estate holdings and their efforts to grow avocado production. I still would be in no rush here, so I’m rating them a hold.

Limoneira’s effort to grow avocado production is going to take years to fully realize, the real estate profit is possible, but the timing there is uncertain. As such, even though the stock is around its 52-week low, I don’t see any obvious catalysts in 2025 or 2026. This is a long-term play, and prices might get lower still between now and when those avocado trees start maturing.

Investors should keep a close eye on that Sunkist merger, because that’s the more immediate matter. The $5 million cost savings will be welcome surely, but depending on how much it drags revenue down it might not result in substantial, meaningful GAAP earnings. With a 7.5¢ quarterly dividend, they too end up with a lower yield than the sector median, so even for the buy and hold crowd, there are likely better options out there at these prices.

data.title: PNC Financial: Long-Term Investors Should Wait For A Pullback
data.description:
PNC Financial (NYSE:PNC) is one of the largest banks in the U.S. and wants to expand its presence in the industry over the coming years, but growth prospects in the near term seem to be somewhat low and its sizable exposure to CRE is concerning, leading to a fair valuation right now. As this is my first coverage of PNC Financial, in this article I do an overview of its business profile and then review its most recent financial performance, to see if it offers value or not for long-term investors within the banking sector.

Business Overview

PNC Financial is a regional bank headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, being one of the largest banking groups in the U.S. Indeed, at the end of last year, its total assets amounted to about $560 billion, being among the largest banks in the U.S. by this measure, even though it’s much smaller than large U.S. banks like Wells Fargo (WFC) or Citigroup (C) which have total assets close or above $2 trillion. Having said that, PNC Financial is among the largest regional banks in the country, having a similar size to Truist Financial (TFC) and slightly smaller than compared to US Bancorp (USB). Its current market value is about $70 billion, and its shares trade on the New York Stock Exchange.

Its business model is diversified across retail banking, corporate and investment banking (CIB), and asset management. This is a distinctive factor compared to other regional banks, which usually are much more concentrated on retail and commercial banking, while PNC Financial has a better business diversification. However, its asset management unit has a small weight on the group, making retail and CIB the two most important segments.

Geographically, the bank has been expanding its presence throughout the country over the past few years, and is nowadays present in the most important markets, both through a physical branch presence and a digital retail offering. Its growth strategy is to continue to expand its physical branch presence over the coming years, aiming to invest about $1.5 billion over the next five years, by opening some 200 new branches and renovating other 1,400 branches.

The bank believes that building scale is essential to have a sustainable business model in the banking industry, a trend that was accelerated since 2023 following the regional banking turmoil. Indeed, customers and businesses started to question the sustainability of smaller banks following the collapse of SVB Financial in March 2023, preferring to do business and take their deposits with larger banks. While PNC Financial benefited from this trend due to its relatively large size among the regional banking space, the U.S. large banks benefited even more, showing that size and scale are important factors in the industry. Therefore, PNC Financial’s strategy is to continue to gain scale in the U.S. banking sector and has also increased marketing spending to create brand awareness nationally.

This means PNC Financial should be able to grow its business by expanding its presence nationwide and also by gaining market share, especially against smaller competitors, plus it may also pursue acquisitions if they make sense financially and from a business strategy standpoint.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, PNC Financial has delivered a relatively stable performance in recent years, as the tailwind of higher rates was offset to some extent by higher deposit costs following the regional banking turmoil in 2023. Despite that, its deposit base has been quite stable following some outflows after Q1 2023, showing that PNC Financial enjoys a good reputation in the banking sector, which is critical for the sustainability of its business model over the long term.

As shown in the previous graph, its total deposits have been quite stable at around $420 billion over the last year, while the average cost of deposits has been on a declining trend following the Fed’s rate cuts initiated last September. According to the bank, its cumulative deposits beta is 51%, showing that PNC Financial’s deposit sensitivity to rates is not great, boding well for its margins.

On the loans side, PNC Financial’s loan book amounted to $317 billion at the end of last quarter, which means its loan-to-deposits (L/D) ratio was 75% in Q1 2025. This is a good level in the banking sector and slightly below the regional bank average that is around 80%, showing that PNC Financial’s business is mainly funded through customer deposits and the bank has low need to finance itself on the wholesale market. This leads to a more sustainable business model over the long term, being a positive feature of its business profile.

As usual in the regional banking sector, PNC Financial is a bank that is significantly exposed to rates, which can be a double-edge sword depending on the rate environment. In the last quarter, net interest income (NII) amounted to nearly $3.5 billion, while non-interest income was above $1.8 billion. Therefore, total revenues were close to $5.45 billion, of which about 64% were generated from NII. This is an acceptable level in the banking sector and lower than compared to other banks that are much more exposed to NII, like Comerica (CMA) or Zions (ZION), being a positive factor on a relative basis during a potential period of lower rates ahead.

Moreover, PNC Financial has shown less sensitivity to rates than it could be expected from a bank that is highly exposed to retail and CIB. Its NII only declined by 1% on a quarterly basis in Q1 2025 and its net interest margin increased by three basis points (bps) to 2.78%, which can be considered a resilient performance. Other revenues had a worse performance, as non-interest revenues declined by 2% on a quarterly basis, explained by lower capital markets and advisory revenues which were impacted by higher market volatility.

Regarding costs, the bank had a good performance considering that operating expenses amounted to less than $3.4 billion in Q1 2025, representing a decline of 3% from the previous quarter. Its efficiency ratio was 62% in the last quarter, an acceptable level compared to its peers, even though this is a level that is not among the most efficient banks in the U.S. and shows that PNC Financial still has some work to do to improve its business efficiency over the coming years.

On the asset quality front, like many regional banks, PNC Financial has a significant exposure to Commercial Real Estate (CRE) even though the bank has been reducing its exposure to CRE in recent quarters, to reduce risk in its balance sheet. Nevertheless, at the end of last quarter, CRE accounted for about 10% of its loan book or about $32 billion, which is a sizable exposure. The most important segments of CRE are multifamily and office loans, which together account for 7% of its total loan book, while other segments like industrial, warehouses, or retail have smaller weights on loans.

The segment that is more problematic is office, but PNC Financial has been reducing exposure to office loans and has increased reserves for loan losses over the past few quarters, being now on a more comfortable position than compared to some quarters ago. Reflecting its more adequate level of reserves related to office, net charge-offs in the office segment declined to only $8 million in Q1 2025 (vs. $62 million in the previous quarter), showing that asset quality in the office segment has stabilized and further losses may be much more limited ahead.

Despite its exposure to CRE and office specifically, PNC Financial’s net charge-off ratio was only 26 bps in Q1 2025, which is a very acceptable ratio compared to peers, showing that the bank’s overall credit quality is good and beyond CRE its loan book seems to be sound.

Its net income in Q1 2025 amounted to $1.4 billion, representing a quarterly decline of 7% and its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the banking sector, was 11.6%. This is an acceptable level of profitability compared to other regional banks but is not among the most profitable banks in the U.S., showing that PNC Financial has further room to improve its profitability by growing its business and achieving higher operating efficiency over the next few years.

Regarding capital, its CET1 ratio was 10.6% at the end of last March, being above its regulatory requirement, but below some of its peers. I don’t see this ratio has being particularly strong, even though it has increased over the past few quarters through retained earnings and is now at a more acceptable level (it was around 10% at the beginning of 2024 and around 9.5% by the middle of 2023).

Despite this relatively weaker capital position than other banks, PNC Financial has returned a good part of earnings to shareholders through dividend and share buybacks, a strategy that is expected to be maintained ahead. During the first quarter of 2025, the bank returned about $800 million to shareholders, representing around 57% of its quarterly earnings. Regarding its dividend, the bank is currently distributing $1.60 per quarter, or $6.40 annually, while current consensus estimates are for the bank to raise its annual dividend to about $6.55 per share related to 2025 earnings. This shows that PNC Financial’s dividend growth prospects are somewhat low ahead. On the other hand, its current dividend yield is about 3.6%, being slightly above the banking sector average even though it’s not a high-dividend yield.

Regarding its valuation, PNC Financial is currently trading at about 1.39x book value, which seems to be a fair multiple for a stable bank like PNC Financial. As can be seen in the next graph, its valuation multiple has traded between 1.1-1.7x book value over the past few years, being nowadays at the middle of its valuation range.

Assuming that interest rates are more likely to go down in the future and macroeconomic uncertainty has increased over the past few weeks due to Trump’s trade war, I think a higher valuation multiple is not much likely in the short term, making PNC Financial’s valuation fair right now.

Conclusion

PNC Financial is a regional bank with an interesting profile due to a higher diversification both geographically and by operating segments than other regional banks, but it has relatively muted growth prospects and sizable exposure to CRE, two factors that don’t bode well for higher profitability ahead. Therefore, I see its current valuation as being fair, and I think investors should wait for a pullback to potentially consider its shares in the future.

data.title: Dividend Investors: Understand Your Risks
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Today I want to talk about "risk". There is a sense out there that "high dividends = high risk" and that often leads investors to believe that they are reducing risk by investing in lower-yielding investments. Is this true?

As with most pieces of bumper sticker wisdom, there is an element of truth to it. Yields get higher as prices are lower, and an increasing risk is one of many reasons why the price of an investment might be lower. If you were holding something that was yielding 5%, and now it's yielding 10%, the odds are high that the risk increased. Essentially, the price of the investment decreased by 50%, and when prices move that significantly, there is likely a reason for it, and that reason is likely that the market believes the investment is now considered higher risk than it was previously. The market isn't always right, but sometimes it is. This is why you can't simply enter the market blindly and buy high-yield investments. You must examine each situation and make your best effort to predict the future outcome of the investment.

On the other hand, if you are buying a company that is already yielding 10%, you're taking a lot less risk than the person who bought that same company at 5%. To be precise, you are buying the same number of shares at half the price, so you are putting half of the capital at risk for the same result, or you are putting the same amount of capital at risk for twice the potential reward. Paying less is lower risk. Collecting more capital back sooner is a lower risk.

Yet, investors often pay high prices for low-yielding investments, believing that they are reducing their risk and improving their future total returns. Are they really?

Today, we are going to imagine that the year is 2007 and two people have accumulated $500,000 and are heading into retirement. Let's meet them.

Income Irene is heading into retirement and is following the Income Method. She has a $500,000 nest egg, and with Social Security, she calculates that she needs to withdraw $30,000 per year to maintain her lifestyle. Yes, she heard that you are only supposed to withdraw 4%, but she isn't really ready to give up her house to downsize. Besides, she is able to construct a portfolio that is expected to produce a yield of 8.6% this year. Irene invests her money in a high-yielding portfolio of stocks similar to what we do at HDO (High Dividend Opportunities).

Cautious Cal is also heading to retirement. Unfortunately, Cal was laid off. He had hoped to grow his portfolio to $750,000 so that he could follow the 4% rule to get the $30,000/year income that he needs. When he was laid off, he had $500,000 in his retirement account. Cautious Cal has heard that high yield is high risk. So, even though he could really use the excess cash, he decided to invest solely in Dividend Aristocrats. These are companies that have raised their dividend every year for 25+ years, even through the Dot-com bust. In fact, the charts for his holdings are fantastic. Over the past 10 years, the Dividend Aristocrat portfolio has had a CAGR of 13.85%, while the S&P 500 has only produced 8.34%. So, while Cautious Cal would have preferred to work for a few more years, he's making the best of it and investing in what he believes are low-risk investments. He buys 47 of the original 57 dividend aristocrat stocks.

Which retiree do you believe is taking less risk?

Selection Methodology:

Team HDO: The Team HDO portfolio was selected from currently held common equity holdings in the HDO Model Portfolio, with the only requirement being that it had to be trading as of January 1st, 2007. It represents a good representation of the Model portfolio with BDCs, REITs, CEFs, MLPs, utilities, and other high-yielding investments. Note that it does include numerous holdings that have cut their dividends over the years, like Annaly Capital (NLY). In fact, by our count, only four of these holdings went from 2007 to 2025 without cutting their dividends. Some are currently underperforming, including holdings such as Macerich (MAC) and Medical Properties Trust (MPW). We made no attempt to hide poor picks or emphasize our best picks. The only litmus test was that the stock was publicly traded as of January 1, 2007. The full list of stocks included in this analysis is available to members and includes 19 holdings that were publicly traded in 2007 and are publicly traded now.

Dividend Aristocrats: We went through the original Dividend Aristocrats list as of 2005. We included every company that was on that list, except those that have gone out of business or are no longer publicly traded.

Total Return

First, here is a look at the portfolios' performance, as conventionally presented: Total Return, measured by the stocks assuming full reinvestment of dividends.

By this measure, Income Irene came up a little short with $2.46 million compared to Cautious Cal's $2.72 million.

That is what people often look at and demand that I admit high yield is high risk. Here is what I look at:

In the year 2024, Income Irene's portfolio produced $180,774 in dividends compared to Cautious Cal at $68,044. Irene is receiving 2.65x more income than Cal. This is actually very close to 2007 when Income Irene's portfolio produced 2.75 times the income that Cautious Cal's portfolio did. Despite 17 years for the Dividend Aristocrats to do their thing, and despite 79% of Irene's holdings cutting dividends at some point, Irene's portfolio maintained its lead in income generation while posting a competitive total return.

But wait, Irene and Cal are both retired. Both of them were withdrawing funds from their portfolio. People love to look at total returns, but total returns don't represent reality unless you are selling your entire portfolio today. In the real world, retirees aren't selling everything today. They are withdrawing a little bit every year to live off of. What is the point of a retirement portfolio if you aren't going to use it to retire?

This is where Portfolio Visualizer is one of the best tools on the Internet to illustrate real retiree behavior, not fantasy portfolios where money is never withdrawn.

High-Yield Outperforms in Real Conditions

Here we assume that both retirees withdrew $30,000 in year one, and they increased their withdrawals with inflation every year thereafter. Why $30k? First, it accounts for approximately 75% of the high-yield portfolio's income, which follows the Income Method. Second, it's a realistic amount for a retiree to want to withdraw.

For a retiree who accumulated $500,000 during their working years, $30,000 per year is a great supplement to their Social Security, helping to fill the gap between what they were earning during their working years. It isn't a pie in the sky, living on a yacht, and partying with rockstars number. It's a reasonable supplement that, for the average retiree, is the difference between scraping by and worrying about the bills and living a reasonably comfortable lifestyle.

When we add withdrawals, with the same stocks in both portfolios and the same time frame, the numbers change significantly.

Suddenly, Team HDO outperforms. Income Irene has an ending portfolio value of $815,704, while Cautious Cal has $790,603.

On the income side, Irene's portfolio produced about $60,000 in 2024, while Cautious Cal's portfolio produced about $20,000.

Adjusted for inflation, the withdrawal was slightly under $47,000. In other words, Income Irene's portfolio is still producing significantly more than she is withdrawing, while Cautious Cal's dividends have always been below his withdrawals. Cal sold $27,000 worth of shares last year to fund his lifestyle. Irene reinvested over $13,000 into new investments.

So, who is taking more risk?

Dividend Cuts

Investors live in fear of dividend cuts. I get it; they aren't fun, and it's definitely nice to avoid them when you can. However, the Team HDO portfolio above had many holdings that cut their dividend over the years. In fact, nearly 80% of the holdings cut their dividend during this investment period. Yet, despite those cuts, Irene's income grew from $40,000 in 2007 to $60,000 in 2024 – a 50% increase!

Her income wasn't immune to volatility. It declined by approximately 25% during the GFC. It declined by approximately 10% during the COVID-19 period. Income Irene did nothing to try to avoid these cuts; she just held through, and in the long run, her income grew 50% even as she withdrew money every year.

Cal thought he was investing more cautiously. However, the GFC was rough on him. His income went from about $15,000 to below $10,000 – a 34% reduction. Keep in mind that his yield in 2007 was 3%. By 2024, Cal's dividend income increased by just 33%.

Irene had more dividend cuts and fewer dividend raises. Yet, the largest reduction in her income was smaller, and Irene's portfolio, in the heart of the GFC, produced more income than Cal's portfolio produced in any year. Over 18 years, the growth of the Dividend Aristocrats couldn't catch up.

Is there a higher risk of a dividend cut among high-yielders? Maybe. Roughly half of the Dividend Aristocrats never cut their dividend, while about 80% of the high-yielders did. However, when an investment yields only 3%, and it cuts, that's a significant deal. However, when an investment yields 8-10%, and it cuts 50%, it still has a higher yield than something that yields 3%.

This is what happened in her portfolio. Yes, she saw cuts, but despite the cuts, she has excess income to reinvest and buy more shares. As the companies resolved their issues, most of them raised their dividends, and Irene owned more shares.

Cal, on the other hand, found himself selling shares every single year. The dividend growth in his portfolio needed to be higher than the dividends lost from the shares he sold. And in many years, it did, but in many, it didn't.

Volatility

Another measure of risk is price volatility. Irene's portfolio did see larger price swings. The largest sell-off was -53.78% compared to Cautious Cal's -47.05%.

We would suggest that psychologically, there isn't a big difference between -54% and -47%. Psychologically, when going through that big drawdown in 2009, Irene was collecting approximately $30,000 in dividends, while Cal was collecting just $10,000 in dividends. The withdrawal plan called for withdrawing $32,000 that year, so Irene had to choose between selling $2,000 worth of stock at unattractive prices or just cutting back spending by $2,000. Cal was faced with selling $22,000 in stock at unattractive prices. Who is more stressed?

But, by the numbers, it is true that the higher-yield stocks were more volatile in price.

Conclusion: The High Risk of Assuming You Can Avoid Risk

Often, I see investors talking about "capital preservation" or avoiding risk. The stock market is not a good place for either one. Yes, some stocks might be less volatile than others, but "less volatile" is very different from "no volatility". In my experience, I see people like Cautious Cal panic. They saw their portfolio value crash 40% when they invested in stocks that they believed were very low risk. He invested in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats – there is no list of stocks that are perceived as lower risk. From a company standpoint, it is a list of very strong companies with excellent historical track records. Their track records are great until they aren't, and they become "fallen angels".

The harsh reality of the stock market is that stock prices are volatile. Prices will swing, and declines of +50% are not unusual. In terms of having the smallest maximum drawdown among all the stocks in both portfolios, it was McDonald's (MCD) at -23%. Who says fast food is bad for your health? But both portfolios have numerous holdings that pulled back over 50%.

Common equities are publicly traded, and public markets are subject to the whims of our neighbors. When our neighbors are fearful, they sell. When prices go down, they become more fearful and they sell more. The herd mentality is strong in the market.

Investing in large companies doesn't protect you from price swings.

Does investing in lower-yielding stocks protect you from dividend cuts? Well, yes and no. It is probably fair to say that higher-yielding investments are going to see more variation in the dividends they pay. That means cuts will be more frequent. Whereas, if you buy a dividend aristocrat, the risks of a cut for any particular company are somewhat lower. However, the impact of a cut is much higher for a dividend aristocrat.

In the high-yield world, we are often talking about RICs (Regulated Investment Companies). This is a corporate structure that means the company won't pay taxes at the corporate level if it distributes substantially all of its taxable income. As a result, these companies are paying out nearly 100% of their taxable income and sometimes more. When taxable income declines, the dividend tends to follow. Of course, taxable income goes up, too, and when it does, the dividend follows.

Whereas a dividend aristocrat might be paying out 30-40% of its taxable income. It has a wider buffer, so when earnings decline, it can just pay an elevated payout for a while. It means fewer cuts, but when they happen, they are brutal. An already low yield gets lower or disappears, and then the share price crashes because the only reason the Cautious Cal's of the world bought something yielding 3% was because they believed the dividend would grow for decades.

So, while buying Dividend Aristocrats reduces the frequency you might experience dividend cuts, the severity of the cuts is much higher. The reason is that when you invest at 3%, you need the company to increase its dividend 10-15%/year to make the investment worthwhile. Even just pausing growth is a huge negative. When you invest at 8%+ you don't need the company to increase the dividend at all. More importantly, you have a much larger cushion in your income to absorb cuts. If you're Cautious Cal, every dividend cut means you have to sell more shares during a bad time to meet your cash needs. If you're Income Irene, your portfolio can absorb a GFC – the worst dividend-cutting period in history – and still produce the cash you need.

It would be a wonderful world if we could avoid every dividend cut and only invest in assets that produce double-digit returns. But we don't live or invest in the land of rainbows and unicorns. We live in the real world, where some investments we make are terrible and many are mediocre. Every time we invest in common equity, we are taking a risk. There is a risk that the price will fall, and a risk that dividends may be cut. We can't predict the future, and neither can the CEOs of the companies we invest in. Mistakes will be made, sometimes by us, sometimes by the companies we invest in.

At the end of the day, the lowest risk is the cash you have in hand right now. The dividends you receive this month lower your risk. It's cash in hand that means you don't have to sell shares to fund your lifestyle. In their efforts to "reduce risk", the Cautious Cals of the world take on one of the highest risks in the market. They rely on selling shares to get the income they need.

Income Irene went through the GFC, and she went through COVID. She saw the prices in her portfolio fluctuate, and she noticed several dividend cuts. Yet, despite all those risks being realized through what were two of the toughest periods for income stocks, her income comfortably covered the amount she wanted to withdraw. She wasn't forced to sell at poor prices. Instead, she was buying while prices were low. If another GFC starts tomorrow, Income Irene won't need to sell a share. Cautious Cal will have to sell more shares if the market is bullish, bearish, or somewhere in between.

It's budgeting 101: Spend less than your income. Spending more than your income is a very high risk. Yet, many plan to do so and think that their choice is somehow "conservative."

I'll invest in high-yield without blinking an eye. Spending more than my income is a risk I would never take.

Income Irene is only spending the money she has collected in dividends. Cautious Cal is selling shares at market prices to fund his lifestyle. Who do you think is taking a higher risk?

data.title: While TLTW Crawled, Our TLT Strategy Sprinted
data.description:
Article Thesis

I can understand the desire of income-oriented investors to secure a double-digit dividend yield through these buy-write ETFs. But when it comes to total return, the landscape is bleak. That is because these ETFs generally implements a mechanical one-month near ATM (At-The-Money) covered call strategy, with bad performances when the underlying fluctuates up and down.

This is the case of iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (BATS:TLTW), with its underlying TLT. I wrote an article at the end of January about TLTW, and we can see a nearly flat total return:

In my article I explained how investors could be significantly better than TLTW, by directly implementing (sometimes simple) options strategies on TLT. For example, my strategy brought ~37% return, as mentioned in a note posted on April 3. So, we could be 100% passive for 1.83% return, or a little bit active for 37% return, a big difference. But more about this strategy in different scenarios later.

Perspective On TLT

iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) invests, as its name suggests, in fixed-rate U.S. treasury securities with remaining maturity of greater than or equal to twenty years:

What I like very much about TLT is that it has very good liquidity, even for options. Not only this, but it has a relatively high IV (Implied Volatility) for a fixed-income ETF, so that we can collect good enough premiums when selling a covered Call, a Put, or even a Put Spread.

However, this volatility is not enough when selling options, we obviously need to have some fundamental perspective, to decide at what underlying price to initiate a position, and the position size.

As usual, opinions are divided, even here at Seeking Alpha: from a very rosy perspective to a not at all rosy one. In my opinion, the determining factors today are:

  • FED rate: and like I said, it’s not necessarily how high (since these very long-term rates are not directly influenced by the FED rate), but for how long. We are already in a wait-and-see mode from the FED with high rates. Long-term, the FED rate is expected to be between 2% and 3%, but if this will be perceived between 3% and 4%, then long-term rates could go up, so TLT could go down. However, could the FED wait several more years? I think it’s unlikely, so perhaps that’s already baked into the price.
  • Inflation and strength of the economy: these tariffs reignited expectations about higher inflation, but here is the thing: this could be indeed an initial push, followed by a moderation, since I can’t see tariffs constantly growing (although nothing is certain, as we could clearly see in these three-four months), so TLT likely incorporates this into the price too.
  • Long-term structural problems: there are more and more voices talking about an unsustainable long-term U.S. deficit, but this is the most uncertain factor, since it’s about a very distant future. Not only that external factors are unknown for such a future, but there could also be different U.S. administrations with different policies during this time.
  • Global bond trends: indeed, there is some pressure this year, especially from Japan. However, Japanese yields (for example, ~2.5% for 20-year bonds) are still far below the U.S. ones. If we look at other developed countries, only the U.K. and New Zealand have relatively high yields like the U.S. (and with about the same credit rating). Then again, it’s likely dependent on the first two factors (FED rate and inflation), and I said I think that is likely baked into the price.

In conclusion, while we could see short-term volatility (and I like that, because I can sell options for interesting premiums), I think that mid-to-long-term TLT likely incorporates these unknowns into the price. That means I can’t see it going too much lower (and staying there), so I am neutral to slightly bullish, or at least not bearish.

TLTW Problems Explained

Like I mentioned in the introduction, TLTW sells each month a covered Call option expiring next month, precisely 2% OTM (Out-of-The-Money). Like I explained in detail in my previous article, this mechanical selling at 2% OTM no matter what circumstances, combined with a short duration, are the main culprits for a bad mid-to-long-term performance:

I detailed in my previous article 8 scenarios, let’s just try to group these scenarios now:

  1. Every time when TLT fluctuates about 2% or less at the end of the month, TLTW outperforms its underlying. That’s because it sells 2% OTM Calls, so it also enjoys price appreciation for these 2%.
  2. TLTW also over-performs with TLT price mostly decreasing (since this decrease is partially offset by the collected premiums from Calls), and this was mainly the case in almost three years since inception, as we can see in the above chart. But this relative over-performance is not a reason for joy at all, since both had negative returns.
  3. Every time when TLT fluctuates more than 3% or above at the end of the month, TLTW under-performs its underlying. That’s because it enjoyed only 2% price appreciation and now it will likely sell a Call option with a strike price too high, with a premium too low, exposed to price reversal.
  4. TLTW also under-performs on constant TLT price increases of over 2%, since it limits its price appreciation at 2%.

Basically, it would make sense to invest in TLTW only for the first scenario (or group of scenarios) above, out of four. And that might be 25% or not, but it’s definitely less than 50% of the cases.

Better Or Much Better Strategies

Strategy #1: A Simple Covered Call

Directly sell a covered Call on TLT (100 TLT shares and 1 short Call). This is one of the simplest strategies with options, so it’s suitable even for beginners. Here are some principles to target a higher return than TLTW:

  • Sell 45 days to two months away those ~2% OTM Call options. For example, a TLT 88 Call Jul 11 can be sold for $65, while a TLT 88 Call Jul 25 for $100, 54% higher for a period only 46% longer.
  • Buy back the Call when it has low extrinsic value, don’t wait until expiration. For example, if that Jul 25 Call sold for $120 will value only $15 on July 1 (let’s say TLT price went down), it's not worth it to keep it more, we should avoid a price reversal, and we maximized our return per month.
  • Be more flexible with strike prices. If we are neutral to slightly bullish (like now), we can sell ~2% OTM. If we are more bullish (perhaps on a further pullback), sell 3% OTM, keeping more space for price appreciation. If we are mostly neutral (perhaps after a TLT price increase), sell an ATM option. If we become neutral to slightly bearish (perhaps after even more price appreciation), sell 1% or 2% ITM (In-The-Money), we want more protection to the downside.

Strategy #2: A Better Bull Put Spread

Sell a Put option and buy a lower strike Put, this is for the same neutral-to-slightly-bullish setup but with better returns, sometimes much better. While not very complex, this is a bit more complex than a covered Call, I would say it requires an intermediate level. For beginners with options, I recommend them to first test it on demo account for about one year.

Here is how I implemented this strategy in January:

  • Short TLT 85 Put Jul 18, received $247
  • Long TLT 82 Put Jul 18, paid $146.

Premium collected: $101.

Cost: $199 = $300 (cash needed to set aside if both Puts will finish ITM) – $101.

As posted in a note on April 3, I closed that position for $28 (due to low extrinsic value), meaning a profit of $73, or ~37% in about two months. And here is how a ~5% movement in the underlying (from ~$88 to ~$92), helped by the passage of time, generated a 37% gain for this strategy! Even more, TLT is now a bit under $86, even before the level from January, so here is what means to close those options early, with low extrinsic value remained, we avoided a price reversal!

And then, as posted in another note from May 12, with TLT down from ~$92 to $86.5, I re-initiated the same strategy, this time short 84 Put, long 81 Put, expiring Nov 21, for $103.

My plan is to:

  • Close again the position if TLT will go up, with low extrinsic value remained. For example, with $30 left, I would close it for $73, another 37%.
  • Increase my exposure through these Put Spreads if TLT will go down (like when buying a stock, we average down), for now I have only about 0.25-0.3x of a normal weight position.

Risks

What if TLT will go below my lower strike price of $81? Like I said when pondering TLT fundamentals, I am neutral to slightly bullish mid-to-long term, while acknowledging possible short-term fluctuations.

Like I described in my initial article, the key here is to close the position early, because at expiration both 84 Put and 81 Put will be ITM, so we’ll close it for -$300 (for a $197 loss). But with, say, three months until expiration, that 81 Put will still have plenty of extrinsic value, so we can close the position for significantly less than $300, then roll it down, eventually by adding more funds. For example:

Interesting, isn’t it? 25% ROI even with TLT slightly down, at ~$81.

If it will go down even more? You got it, I will roll it down once again. That’s why I have only ~0.3x of a normal weight position now, so I can add more funds on its way down. But I might also add more if I sense an improvement in fundamentals.

In theory, there is also the scenario that TLT will go down and I will just take the loss and walk away. In the example above, with TLT at ~$81 in August, we would have about $80 loss, or ~41%, not shy, but… only a cataclysm in the U.S. economy could make me stop rolling down the strategy and incur losses.

Takeaway

TLT has a good liquidity and relatively high implied volatility for a fixed-income ETF. This makes it ideal for selling options, through either simple strategies like covered Call, or slightly more complex like bull Put Spread. While acknowledging possible short-term volatility, I'm neutral to slightly bullish on TLT mid-to-long term, I think most of the determining factors (FED rate, inflation, global bond market) are likely baked into the price.

Buy-write ETFs like TLTW offer high yields, but ugly mid-to-long term total returns due to rigid covered call strategies, especially during volatile markets.

Selling a covered Call is an interesting neutral-to-bullish strategy, but in order to over-perform TLTW, we could directly sell covered Calls on TLT, with longer duration and more flexible strike prices or redemption dates.

An even better neutral-to-bullish strategy is a bull Put Spread. The strategy that I implemented at the end of January and closed in April (due to low extrinsic value) returned ~37%, compared to less than 2% for TLTW. In May, I re-initiated a position, planning to close it if TLT will go up (with low extrinsic value) or roll it down by adding more funds if TLT will go down.

data.title: ONEOK: Now Is The Time To Buy Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My investing strategy involves blending quality and valuation.

That's because well-run companies with proven business models tend to outperform expectations over the long haul. This can lead to market-beating total returns over time.

Yet, the importance of valuation can't be overstated, either. The same stock can produce very different total returns, depending on the valuation.

ONEOK (NYSE:OKE) is arguably a case study of how valuation plays a key role in total returns. When I last covered OKE with a hold rating in March 2024, I liked its healthy growth outlook. The BBB credit rating from S&P was another positive. The safe dividend was a plus as well. However, these were all counteracted by what, I thought, was an excessive valuation at the time.

Skip ahead 15 months, and I'm now upgrading shares back to a buy rating for the first time since November 2023. The company's combination of upcoming synergies and capital projects coming online are meaningful future tailwinds. OKE's balance sheet remains investment-grade. I also estimate that shares are now priced at a double-digit percentage discount to fair value.

ONEOK Has A Bright Future Ahead

On April 29th, OKE shared its first-quarter earnings report for the period ended March 31st, 2025. The company's revenue soared 68.2% over the year-ago period to $8.04 billion during the quarter. That was $682 million ahead of the Seeking Alpha analyst consensus in the quarter.

What was behind this significant top-line growth for the first quarter?

According to CFO Walter Hulse's opening remarks during the Q1 2025 Earnings Call, this was mostly due to a full quarter of adjusted EBITDA from the EnLink and Medallion acquisitions.

Last August, OKE acquired Global Infrastructure Partners' entire 43% interest in EnLink Midstream for $14.90 per unit and 100% of the interest in the managing member for $300 million. This was a total cash consideration of $3.3 billion. Five months later, OKE acquired the remaining publicly held interests in a share swap, with EnLink shareholders receiving 0.1412 shares of OKE for each share that they owned.

In the same press release last August, OKE also announced that it purchased Medallion Midstream outright from GIP for $2.6 billion (a 6.3x 2025 EBITDA multiple, including anticipated synergies). This was the largest privately held crude gathering and transportation system in the Permian's Midland Basin.

The company's rationale for these moves was multi-faceted and included the following reasons:

  1. The assets were complementary to OKE's existing Permian NGL and crude infrastructure platform, offering new services for producers in the region.
  2. EnLink expanded and extended its footprint in Mid-Continent, North Texas, and Louisiana.
  3. The transactions were also expected to be accretive to EPS and FCF upon closing, with annual synergies of between $250 million and $450 million within three years.

A 15% year-over-year increase in Rocky Mountain region NGL raw feed throughput volumes also contributed to the top-line growth during the first quarter. As did a 7% increase over the year-ago period in Rocky Mountain region natural gas volumes processed in the quarter.

This is what led OKE's adjusted EBITDA to surge 23.2% year-over-year to $1.78 billion for the first quarter. That was only partially neutralized by a 4.6% uptick in the diluted average share count to 612.5 million during the quarter.

Looking ahead, OKE should have the catalysts needed to maintain strong growth. As I outlined in a listicle with Brad Thomas, OKE recently completed its West Texas NGL Pipeline and Elk Creek NGL Pipeline expansions.

As pumping stations for the former are finished in the middle of this year for the former, that will bring capacity up from 515,000 barrels per day to 740,000 barrels per day. The availability of the full power supply for the latter in the middle of 2025 will increase capacity from 435,000 barrels per day to 575,000 barrels per day.

OKE is also in the middle of connecting the EnLink and Medallion systems. As these projects are completed, more of those synergies that I noted earlier can materialize.

Recently announced joint ventures with MPLX LP (MPLX) can lengthen OKE's growth even further. In February, the company announced definitive agreements to construct a new large-scale 400,000-barrel-per-day LPG export terminal in Texas City, Texas, as well as a new 24-inch pipeline from Mont Belvieu, Texas, to the new terminal. The former will have a 50/50 ownership split and be operated by MPLX, while the latter will have an 80/20 split and be operated by OKE.

This is why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that OKE's OCF per share will grow by 16.4% in 2025 to $9.81. Another 14.3% rise in OCF per share to $11.20 is expected for 2026.

OKE's financial positioning is respectable, too. As of March 31st, 2025, the company had $31.7 billion in net debt on its balance sheet. Against the $8.225 billion in midpoint adjusted EBITDA forecast for 2025, this would be a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of around 3.9x. As the company grows and synergies are realized, Hulse expects it will trend toward its target of 3.5x in 2026.

OKE also had no borrowings outstanding under its $3.5 billion facility, which gives it the ability for opportunistic bolt-on acquisitions. For these reasons, the company possesses a BBB credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to OKE's Q1 2025 Earnings Press Release and OKE's May 2025 Investor Presentation).

Double-Digit Upside To Fair Value

Since my previous article, shares of OKE have posted 11% total returns to the 18% total returns of the S&P 500 index (SP500). The more modest performance over that time combined with OKE's smart acquisitions has changed the rating dynamic for me. These factors have swung the valuation from a double-digit percentage overvaluation to a similar level of undervaluation.

OKE's shares are trading at a current-year P/OCF ratio of 8.4. For more perspective, this is below the 10-year average P/OCF ratio of 9.7, according to data from FAST Graphs. The forward 12-month P/OCF ratio of 7.9 is an even more compelling value.

Overall, I believe that a reasonable fair value estimate for OKE is a standard deviation below the 10-year average P/OCF ratio. That would equate to a P/OCF ratio of 8.7.

This is because OKE's fundamentals appear to be holding up quite well. The annual forward OCF per share growth consensus of 7% is comparable to the average of approximately 6.5% in recent years.

My rationale for a downward rerating in the valuation multiple has to do with the historically low interest rate environment over much of the past decade. The 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yielded an average of 2.5% over this time per Macrotrends. Moving forward, I think that the 10-year yield will settle in the high 3% range.

Over the past 10 years, OKE has traded at an investment spread of approximately 775 basis points to the 10-year U.S. Treasury (10.3% OCF yield versus 2.5% treasury yield). Using that same spread and a high 3% 10-year yield, this is how I arrive at my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.7.

As of now (June 10th, 2025), the calendar year 2025 is 44% complete. That leaves another 56% of 2025 and 44% of 2026 yet to come in the next 12 months. This is how I arrive at a forward 12-month OCF per share input of $10.42.

Plugging this in with my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.7, I compute a fair value of $91 a share. Relative to the current $82 share price, that's equivalent to a 10% discount to fair value.

If OKE lives up to the growth consensus and reverts to fair value, it could deliver an 18% cumulative total return through June 2026 (17% annualized). Through 2030, the midstream operator could post 10%+ annual total returns.

A Secure Dividend With Room To Grow

OKE's 5% forward dividend yield is on the low end for the midstream industry. However, it matches the energy sector's median forward yield. This garners respective C+ and C grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield.

OKE's dividend looks to be safe. The company's payout ratio is set to be in the mid-70% range for 2025. That's why The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal assigns respective 0.5% and 2% risks of dividend cuts in average and severe recessions, respectively. This also tracks with the B+ grade that the Quant System assigns to OKE for overall dividend safety.

OKE also scores decent marks for dividend growth. In the years ahead, the Quant System anticipates forward annual dividend growth of 4.2%. In my view, that's a sensible expectation. This is because OKE itself is targeting at least 3%-4% annual dividend growth. That's behind the B- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth.

This should help OKE to extend its 3-year dividend growth streak in the years ahead. That's better than the sector median of 2 years, and enough for the Quant System to issue a B grade for overall dividend consistency.

Risks To Consider

OKE's fundamentals are encouraging. However, the company faces risks that could set its investment thesis back or even break it.

OKE is known for being active on the M&A front. Thus far, these acquisitions have generally panned out well. The risk is that the anticipated synergies and benefits of recent acquisitions like EnLink and Medallion may not be fully realized. If this happens, that could negatively impact OKE's growth prospects.

Another risk to the company is counterparty risk. More than 90% of OKE's EBITDA base is expected to be fee-based for 2025. This limits the company's sensitivity to fluctuations in commodity prices.

The caveat is that financial results could still be negatively impacted if energy commodities experience a prolonged and severe bear market. Such an event could cause some customers to experience financial difficulties and default on their obligations to OKE.

An additional risk to the midstream operator is that its key growth projects may encounter regulatory delays or cost overruns. If this happens, it could also hurt OKE's growth potential.

One final risk to the company is the possibility of a major cyber breach. If this happens, OKE's operations could be disrupted. Sensitive customer data could also be compromised. The fallout from this type of event could include customer lawsuits and fines from regulators.

Summary: A Core Holding In My Portfolio

OKE is my 18th-biggest investment holding and comprises 1.6% of my portfolio. The company's overall fundamentals picture is promising.

OKE's ongoing capital projects and expected synergies from acquisitions are poised to keep generating growth. The company also enjoys an investment-grade credit rating. OKE's dividend is well-covered and positioned for future growth. Shares are also moderately undervalued right now. That's why I'm upgrading shares back to a buy rating.

data.title: Artisan High Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Discussion

Our portfolio outperformed the ICE BofA US High Yield Index during the quarter. From an asset class perspective, the most notable contributor was security selection in the corporate bond segment. The portfolio’s allocation to bank loans detracted marginally during the quarter on a relative basis but contributed positively to performance on an absolute basis. By rating, the portfolio benefited from security selection in B and CCC-rated debt while an underweight to BB-rated credit detracted from returns. Across sectors, the most notable contributors included security selection in media and telecommunications, while security selection in consumer goods detracted.

Investing Environment

Market narratives shifted materially in Q1 as widespread views of continued economic strength and a pro-growth administration earlier in the quarter gave way to rising investor anxiety over economic growth. Sentiment change accelerated notably in March as increased rhetoric around tariffs began to weigh on investor confidence. After a positive January, the S&P 500® Index peaked in mid-February before falling in March and ending the quarter down 4.3%. Meanwhile, Treasury market volatility continued as the 10-Year Treasury yield reached a high of 4.79% in mid-January before falling nearly 60bps as the quarter progressed, ending March at 4.21%. The volatility in Treasury yields reflected evolving investor expectations around the path of economic growth and monetary policy. At the end of 2024, the Fed funds futures market was pricing in less than two cuts by year-end 2025. By the end of March, more than three cuts were expected by the end of the year.

Encouragingly, credit markets exhibited resiliency in the face of recent market stress and outperformed equities during the quarter, with particularly strong outperformance in March. The ICE BofA US High Yield Index gained 0.9% for the quarter, even as spreads widened 63bps in Q1. In addition, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index gained 0.6% for the quarter with material outperformance over the high yield index in March. As we have previously discussed, the level of absolute yields offered by credit markets continued to provide investors with what we believe to be an attractive cushion, as coupon income more than offset price declines during the quarter. We continue to believe the relative resilience of credit versus equities from a total return perspective in Q1 is another testament to the diversification benefits that credit markets can bring to portfolios.

In a reversal from the second half of 2024, lower rated credit underperformed higher rated during the quarter. The ICE BofA CCC and Lower US High Yield Index fell 0.7% while the ICE BofA BB US High Yield Index gained 1.5%. However, within CCCs there was material dispersion of returns across sectors during the quarter, as those sectors perceived more insulated from tariffs—such as insurance brokers—posted positive returns while other segments, such as retail, fell over 3%. Of particular note is that the distressed component of the market—a material driver of high yield returns in the second half of 2024—fell over 1.8% in the quarter and at time of writing, continues to lag the broader market in April. As discussed in prior letters, the distressed component of the market was bid up in 2024 particularly after the Federal Reserve began its easing cycle but has now notably underperformed in 2025 amid increased investor concerns surrounding economic growth.

At time of writing, market volatility has picked up significantly in April following the unveiling of reciprocal tariffs and continued trade war escalations. At peak year-to-date drawdown on April 8, the S&P 500® Index had fallen 15.0% while the high yield index and loan index had fallen 1.8% and 0.8%, respectively. Given historical downside market capture relationships between credit and equities, investors would have expected higher drawdowns from our market. We believe the evolution of credit markets in recent years, combined with strong technicals, helped provide improved downside protection versus equities in this most recent bout of volatility. As we have previously discussed at length, the high yield market has high-graded over the past 15 years, and we believe that risky issuance is now more frequently financed in the private credit market rather than the broadly syndicated market. In addition, attractive yield levels continued to entice technical demand from buyers, who have viewed recent spread widening as a buying opportunity.

Fundamentally, the high yield bond and leveraged loan markets entered this most recent period of volatility from a position of strength, with a declining near-term maturity wall and stable-to-improving credit metrics. According to recent reports from JPMorgan, high yield balance sheets in 2025 continued to be in a “strong state” with improving interest coverage ratios and declining leverage. Similarly, a year-end report from JPMorgan noted a swath of metrics for the US loan market “stabilizing” in recent quarters, with potential for further improvement from Federal Reserve rate cuts. Finally, as discussed last quarter, companies took advantage of more favorable capital markets conditions in 2024 to address maturities, with the amount of debt maturing in 2025 and 2026 falling materially as a percentage of the overall bond and loan market on a year-over-year basis. Overall, the fundamental strength of today’s credit market gives us a degree of confidence that most companies in our market can manage through what may be a more volatile period in 2025.

Portfolio Positioning

During the quarter, our leveraged loan allocation increased roughly 4% as we continued to find attractive idiosyncratic credits in this market. Our most notable loan purchase in Q1 was the addition of a social media platform, which we initially purchased at an attractive discount to par while benefiting from above-market coupons. The business has rightsized its cost structure since it was taken private in 2022 with improving profitability, and we believe the name offers compelling total return potential with ample cushion, as publicly reported valuations imply an attractive loan-to-value (LTV). At time of writing, we continued to find compelling new investment opportunities in the loan market as a result of April’s market volatility, as select assets in this market offer an attractive combination of discounts to par and what we perceive to be ample compensation for true level of credit risk.

Recent market volatility serves as a useful reminder of the benefits of our differentiated approach and process. As noted above, sector dispersion has increased in 2025 and particularly in April (as of the time of writing). As we entered 2025, our emphasis on business quality—consistent with our process since inception—led us to focus our portfolio on sectors like insurance (brokers), leisure (cruise lines), media and telecommunications. On a relative basis, we believe these sectors are more insulated from tariff concerns by the very nature of their businesses. Notably, what we did not own is just as important as what we did. For example, the energy sector has lagged materially in 2025 with declining macro sentiment weighing on oil prices; at the end of 2024, we held less than 3% exposure in the segment. Consistent with our philosophy and process, when the market is trading relatively tight (such as the end of 2024) we prefer cash flowing assets with less cyclicality, as markets trading close to par typically offer little upside for cyclical risk, such as energy credit.

The quarter also served as a useful reminder of our approach around credit quality. We have historically held above-market weights to assets rated CCC, as we believe certain market segments (such as insurance brokers) continued to be fundamentally misrated by rating agencies. Unsurprisingly, our CCC-rated exposure added value in Q1 as our CCCs outperformed the broader rating category given our emphasis on business quality. It’s also worth highlighting that our CCC exposure overall had been declining in recent quarters as we refocused the CCC segment as credit markets rallied. For example, at the end of 2022 our portfolio held approximately 26% in CCCs; as markets tightened in 2023 and 2024, this allocation had been reduced to 22% at the end of February.

We remain on the hunt for attractive assets trading at discounted prices. At time of writing, there is not yet broad-based dislocation in our market as a result of April’s volatility, though we are beginning to see select opportunities. As always, we will apply a lens of business quality and perform rigorous due diligence when selecting assets for the portfolio and our investors.

Perspective

In a span of just a few months, prevailing market narratives have dramatically shifted. At time of writing, investors are hotly debating the potential for an economic slowdown leading to a recession. Market volatility has considerably increased, and equity markets remained underwater year-to-date. We believe credit markets can provide investors attractive diversification to equity risk while current valuations offer a more attractive entry point than at the start of the year.

From a portfolio perspective, we believe the prospect of further volatility creates opportunity for discerning buyers such as us. While there has yet to be true market dislocation, we are beginning to identify attractively discounted assets offering compelling return potential as a result of the recent market moves. We retain a high bar for inclusion in the portfolio and continue to emphasize business quality and fundamental issuer strength.
