data.title: StoneCo: A Brazilian Growth Powerhouse
data.description:
Despite significant volatility caused by the environment, StoneCo (STNE) nearly tripled its revenue from 2020 and also became profitable, in not the best scenario. Now, the company targets a significant ramp-up in profit which could potentially make this significantly undervalued even for a Brazilian stock - which tends to be cheaper due to country-related perceived risk and economics, as I'll mention below. I bought StoneCo recently, and I believe they still have significant potential given both their own fundamentals, as well as Brazil's and Latin America's long-term tailwinds.

Financials

STNE's revenue developed significantly even with not the most successful acquisitions (which they are now looking into selling) on the back of strong demand for Brazil's fintech sector. This is visible for companies like PagSeguro (PAGS) as well, which are a very similar competitor that is also worth looking into - which I also own.

The net income continued to develop last year, reaching a 16.6% adjusted margin and up 23.1% in Q1 2025 YoY, which is actually in line with PAGS' 5-year average margin of 15.43% and generally fair for the fintech sector. On such significant revenue and net income growth rates, maintaining this margin can result in massive returns.

Last year, they managed to make around $395 million in adjusted net income, which is not amazing for a $3.7 billion market cap now, but was an adjusted PE of ~5.5 back in early 2025 when I first bought. Now, this is not exactly amazing for Brazil, with many Brazilian stocks trading at ratios even as low as 2 or 3 if we look even at their largest public company - Petrobras (PBR) - but we have to take into account the potential to grow, which I will mention below.

We see growth rates in the high 30-40s in pretty much everything, but the stock is still up only ~5% over the past year, although already recovering significantly from the drop they had during this time.

Similarly, their number of clients also keeps recording very significant CAGRs, with 25% in MSMB payments and 60% in banking! Long-term, the number of micro/small/medium businesses should rise as the environment improves following the post-pandemic shock, offering significant potential for STNE to continue delivering this kind of client growth.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a very good position, with current assets above the total liabilities, including their ~$443 million in debt (BRL 2,471 billion). What's left is about $2.06 billion in equity on a $3.7 billion market cap, which isn't the best, but we are looking at a tech company. In this case, the equity is not as significant as with mining companies, for example, because certain digital assets can't be added to the balance sheet unless they are acquired through M&As (e.g. the apps, websites, brand value, etc.). However, this was close to a PB ratio of 1 at the beginning of the year, when I think there was potential to see some "hidden" value in this balance sheet.

Risks & Opportunities

Back in late 2023, StoneCo announced plans to multiply the profit as much as eightfold by 2027, reaching R$4.3 billion (US$771.4 million) in adjusted net profit! They even increased the gross targets at the beginning of the year, backed by very solid results. This comes after beating their R$1.9 billion target in 2024 (delivered R$2.2 billion, 41.3% up YoY) and so far, being on track to reach their 2025 targets!

So, reaching their initial 2027 adj. net profit target of $771.4 million would currently put them at a PE ratio of ~4.8 even after the stock went up, which is very low for any tech stock growing like this, even for Brazil.

Even now, they see massive potential in their total addressable market, with market shares as low as <1% in credit. They talk about the huge potential to expand in the Brazilian market, and I have to add here the potential to expand in the whole continent. Combine it with rate cuts and a friendlier environment for business, and we can probably see them continue to grow like this for many years ahead.

Now, I have to mention that the environment - as much potential as there is in Brazil - is very competitive. Don't think only about PAGS or other local suppliers - you have to remember that this market could be a point of interest for any global or especially American company. Margins can fall, competition can heat up - it's ultimately very dynamic.

It can also be an opportunity because they are relatively very tiny companies and even someone like PayPal (PYPL) or SoFi (SOFI) - on top of Amazon (AMZN) or Apple (AAPL) - could be interested in an acquisition. You know, for PayPal, for example, that's like a 7% increase in revenue for maybe not even 5% of the market cap if they want to issue stock. So, it's something to consider by default, but we have to add the potential to grow on top. That 7% could become 10% in a year or 20% in half a decade, especially if PayPal uses its money, popularity and know-how to develop it.

Plus, there is potential to develop in the region beyond just Brazil. Amazon and the others don't seem to focus that much on South America, despite the potential being very attractive. This can allow companies like StoneCo to continue developing once they solidify a stronger position in Brazil, unlocking decades of potential growth on top of Brazil's own potential.

But, of course, there is political and economic risk, and we can never know what can happen with Trump and BRICS that can really deteriorate the environment. To respond to the effects of the pandemic, Brazil initially cut the interest rates to about 2.5%, but then hiked them significantly until getting to ~15% today, marking some of their highest ratios since 2008. This is seen as a negative thing in the market as well, with Citi recently downgrading STNE and PAGS due to the recent rate hikes.

The country generally raises them during times of economic issues/uncertainty and does the opposite when the global environment normalizes. This could also be caused by the fact that they depend significantly on the price of commodities such as iron, oil or sugar, which are affected when the economy is hurt. Rate hikes are actually pretty bad for StoneCo overall, with most of their revenue coming from transactions, and other services, which are affected during times of high interest rates, which are a tool used to slow down the economy. However, if the long-term tailwind of lower and lower rates continue, this could boost the economy by making debt cheaper and increasing disposable income, basically translating into more demand for StoneCo's services.

Shareholder Returns & Warren Buffett

Although they don't pay a dividend, they did massive buybacks and even ramped up their program to R$2,000 billion, the equivalent of ~$358.8 million, or a nearly 10% yield! This ramp-up actually came later last year, when the yield would've been >13% - although this is not limited to one year.

Also, a note about Warren Buffett buying and then selling this: I don't think it's very relevant. I think he only has control over the bigger transactions, and it's possible that he has no idea they even bought this. I'm not really following what these types of investors do, and they are after all huge companies that aren't as free to buy whatever they want. And as a note, if they bought the whole company, it would cost Berkshire (BRK.B)/(BRK.A) like 1-2% of their cash and equivalents, based on the latest numbers.

If it was his personal portfolio, sure, then it's a much better indicator, and if he bought, then it's very clear what he thinks about them, but this should always be at most an idea for us to look into, not something to blindly follow.

Valuation

Using a discounted cash flow (DCF) approach, I estimate StoneCo's intrinsic value based on a starting FCF of only $300 million (excluding working capital volatility), growing at 10% annually for the next five years, with a long-term growth rate of only 3% beyond that - although the real potential would still be high. I'm using a discount rate of 8.5%, which, I think, is fair for the company's risk profile (Brazil + fintech), with an improving profitability. Assuming 271.5 million shares outstanding and no net debt, my calculation puts the total equity value at around $7.41 billion, or approximately $27.29 per share. However, let's see some medium-term sensitivity scenarios below to get an idea about the possible volatility we could see.

Scenario 1

In a bad scenario with competition ramping up, let's say they develop but the net income only reaches around $200 million on average due to a much lower margin of ~5% even if they continue to grow the revenue at a CAGR of 20% until 2027. For a ratio of 6, which is very low for such a company and below the current levels, that's a market cap of $1.2 billion. That's a price of $4.04, meaning a loss of ~70%, although it can be better if they continue the buybacks. But, at the same time, that's like half of the lowest point ever and the price to book ratio would be around 0.5, so maybe someone could buy them and mitigate some of the loss.

Scenario 2

In a second scenario, let's say that due to the competition, they remain at ~$400 million. This could be possible even if they are growing the revenue at a CAGR of 25%, but with a lower margin of ~8.5%, so their ability to turn a profit remains key. With the buybacks and a slightly better market sentiment, let's say the ratio is at 12. That's a market cap of $4.8 billion. Meaning a price of $16.19 on the current number of shares, for a ~17% profit, although this was as high as 70% if bought 6 months ago. Assuming the effect of buybacks, we could potentially be looking at a 25-30% profit if we adjust the number of shares.

Scenario 3

Now, in a better scenario, let's say they manage to reach the CEO's target and get to $750 million (which is a bit below it). For a ratio of 15, which is justified by the very high-growth rate that this implies (~8x net income from 2023 to 2027) that's a market cap of $11.25 billion, meaning a price of $37.88, for ~175% in profit in a couple of years, or potentially over 200% with the buybacks.

This can even be much better if they ramp up the buybacks and could also be possible, especially if they repay some debt. Plus, there is potential to develop internationally, so that $750 million could become maybe even more than a billion in the long term.

Conclusion

So, overall quite an interesting company that's still at a good price even after it went up, in my opinion, and I believe the current price still offers a good risk-reward potential. Healthy, can make very good money and I think they entered into the next stage of their development as a business when profits start to show. If they reach their target, this could be an easy multibagger, especially if they keep up the buybacks and the market sentiment improves. Plus, they offer exposure to Brazil and emerging markets, and they have potential to develop a lot in the region. But, remember that it remains a very competitive environment, and even if that also means the potential to be bought, there is this constant pressure in a very dynamic market.

data.title: NIO: Too Cheap To Dismiss
data.description:
A highly competitive electric-vehicle market in China is taking a toll on the financial results of NIO Inc. (NYSE:NIO) (OTCPK:NIOIF). The EV company reported disappointing earnings for 1Q25 and NIO missed profit as well as sales estimates for the first quarter. NIO reported a substantial increase in its operating losses YoY, raising questions about the company’s profit trajectory.

In my last review of NIO I concluded with a stock classification of ‘Strong Buy’ primarily because the electric-vehicle company was growing at a quick pace, in particularly as far as ONVO deliveries were concerned.

While the profit backdrop leaves something to be desired, NIO is profiting from substantial delivery growth for both its premium brand as well as its relatively new ONVO and Firefly vehicles. Consequently, I think that NIO’s growth justifies a continued investment in the electric-vehicle company, despite a dissatisfying near-term profit outlook.

ONVO And Firefly Deliveries Are Scaling Up, Profit Situation Remains Suboptimal

NIO is growing, that’s the good news, and the EV company is doing quite well here, actually. In 1Q25, NIO delivered 42,094 electric-vehicles to its customers, reflecting a growth jump of 40.1% YoY. Catalyzing this growth were new deliveries from NIO’s new EV brands ONVO and Firefly. ONVO started to scale up deliveries throughout the fourth quarter whereas Firefly brought its first compact EVs to market in April 2025.

Deliveries for NIO’s Firefly brand have seen some respectable momentum in May 2025 which is when the company delivered to its customers 3,680 electric-vehicles. This reflected an increase from just 231 units in April 2025.

ONVO and Firefly deliveries together accounted for a whopping 43% of total May deliveries with Firefly deliveries having a 16% share, up from just 1% in the prior month.

NIO is clearly tapping into a promising market here if it can realize such eye-popping growth in a very short period of time and it attests the hunger in the market for more affordable EVs. That being said, NIO’s stock price is suppressed by the company’s weak profitability.

My main beef with NIO is that the electric-vehicle company is burning through too much money which is predominantly related to the start of multiple, lower cost electric-vehicle brands, such as ONVO and Firefly. This strategy has created a situation for the electric-vehicle company in which it has to accept considerable operating and net losses that have not only not narrowed, but actually widened, in light of a substantially higher deliveries.

Despite this higher delivery volume and considerable effort to broaden the company’s product appeal by venturing into segments with lower margins, operating losses have increased substantially for NIO in the first quarter.

In 1Q25, NIO had operating losses of RMB6,418.1 million (US$884.4 million), up from RMB5,394.1 million (US$747.1 million) in the first quarter of 2024. Thus, NIO’s operating losses increased by 19% YoY, a sign that the company might not be able to achieve profitability even if its deliveries continue to soar.

As a consequence, NIO is unfortunately at risk of having to raise new equity which poses a risk for shareholders, for obvious reason. New stock sales in the market are poised to dilute investors which could further be a considerable weight on the electric-vehicle company. Moreover, electric-vehicle competition in China is fierce as a brutal fight over market share has taking hold in the last couple of years.

Based on data from EVboosters, about 400 EV brands have stopped selling their electric-vehicles in the last seven years and NIO, despite its losses, has been able to survive up to this point. Now it will be up to management to balance growth with its needs for a more sustainable cost structure. If management can deliver, the stock has a good chance to sell for a higher profit multiple in the future.

NIO’s Stock Still Highly Competitively Valued

I have made the argument in my last piece on the electric-vehicle company that NIO was undervalued based on sales, but NIO’s higher operating losses are still a drag on its valuation multiple. With operating losses also widening substantially YoY, NIO has not yet found a recipe in order to mitigate those operating losses.

NIO presently has a market valuation of $7.1 billion, which is about the same market valuation that Lucid Motors (LCID) has. NIO is selling at a rock-bottom price-to-sales ratio of 0.44x which is substantially below the peer group average of 1.69x, thanks to the company's inability to narrow its operating losses. Peer EV companies in the U.S. are selling for sales multiples of more than 2.0x.

The valuation ratios in the chart below are based on estimated sales for 2026. NIO is anticipated to see 33% sales growth YoY next year compared to 40% for Rivian Automotive Inc. (RIVN) and 95% for Lucid Motors, so NIO has the slowest expected growth, and a valuation multiple that is more than 70% below its peers in the U.S.

NIO has a potential pathway to double its market valuation, in my view, if investors see progress in terms of NIO lowering its operating losses. A doubling of its sales multiple would reflect a price-to-sales ratio of 0.9x in which case NIO would still be significantly cheaper than either Rivian Automotive or Lucid Motors.

That being said, reducing operating losses might be the fastest way for NIO’s management to catalyze a change in investor perception about the EV company.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be More Bearish

The operating loss trend obviously is a bit of a red flag and I wished NIO was more determined in terms of cutting down its costs. In my view, inflated operating losses, related to multiple simultaneous brand launches, are a negative for the overall still bullish investment thesis.

I think that NIO’s delivery upside, particularly as it relates to ONVO and Firefly, is still substantial, however, which is why I am reaffirming my ‘Strong Buy’ stock classification for this EV company.

My Takeaway

It takes a strong stomach to stick with NIO after years of losses and the lack of a turnaround in terms of profitability can be at times quite disheartening.

That being said, I think that NIO’s robust electric-vehicle growth is evidence of the competitiveness of its products in a very competitive and crowded market.

In my view, NIO needs to prioritize cost controls over delivery and production growth in order to be able to entice investors to buy into its electric-vehicle vision.

In terms of valuation, NIO’s stock still very competitively valued, in my view, and offers investors re-rating upside if it can manage to lower its operating costs moving forward.

data.title: Correction Alert: Popular 7-14% Yields Poised For A Sharp Drop
data.description:
Investing in high-yield stocks that are backed by broadly diversified portfolios—mostly loans—at first may seem like a good way to generate attractive and dependable passive income. In fact, I do not disagree with that approach at all, as many funds and companies have successfully pursued that strategy over time. However, paying a premium to net asset value along with fees for the management of that portfolio—especially if the loans are low-quality—right when the economy is facing significant uncertainty and there is a reasonable chance that we could be on the cusp of a recession, I think is a poor approach for setting yourself up for outsized total returns. As a result, in this article, I am sounding the warning on two high-yielding investments that are very popular and yield between 7% and 14%, as I think they are due for a sharp pullback in their stock prices in the not-too-distant future.

High Yield #1

The first stock I want to talk about is the 7% yielding Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN). It is a very popular business development company (BIZD) that has a phenomenal long-term track record of creating value for shareholders. However, today it trades at a nearly 80% premium to its net asset value, which is well above its historic average price-to-NAV ratio.

Additionally, it is quite sensitive to a recession because it only has about 65% of its portfolio invested in senior secured loans and its counterparties are generally middle-market companies, which tend to underperform during periods of economic distress. While MAIN has successfully paid and even grown its dividend over time while supplementing its base payout with generous special dividends, I think that its current premium to NAV is excessive, especially when compared to its historic average price-to-NAV ratio.

This is also illustrated by the fact that its price-to-NAV ratio and its NAV per share have collapsed during past market crashes. For example, look at the COVID-19 crash in 2020, where it also traded at around 1.8x its NAV before crashing to trade at a steep discount to NAV even as its NAV also declined sharply.

If this were to happen again, MAIN would very likely experience a huge decline in shareholder value in a very short period of time, making it a high-risk bet at the moment.

High Yield #2

Meanwhile, the 14% yielding opportunity that, I think, is poised for a sharp drop is the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI). While its 14% yield with monthly payouts, just like MAIN has monthly payouts, is quite attractive and its distribution track record has also been phenomenal, with it never cutting its payout dating all the way back to 2012, along with some supplemental dividends along the way, I do not think it is a particularly attractive total return investment right now.

For starters, it trades at a hefty 12.4% premium to its net asset value while also charging a fairly high fee of 2.18% without even counting its interest expense, which is quite significant given its substantial leverage. Additionally, the 30.83% leverage ratio that it currently has meant that any declines in the underlying value of its assets would be further pronounced on the net asset value of the fund, which would likely lead to further declines in shareholder performance. This is especially concerning given that only about 8% of the fund is invested in investment-grade opportunities, with the vast majority being B or unrated and less than 10% being rated BB. As such, there is a strong probability that in the event of an economic downturn, PDI would suffer considerable NAV erosion. In fact, all we have to do is look back to past history to see that the price to NAV premium also gets erased along with experiencing a deep plunge in NAV when the economy hits a stumbling block. For example, this happened during the COVID crash in a manner quite similar to what happened to MAIN during that period.

As a result, I think that PDI is also a poor choice right now for total return-oriented investors from a risk-adjusted standpoint, even if the distribution remains sustainable for now.

Investor Takeaway

While MAIN and PDI have impressive track records as high-yield machines that have also been in the market for a long time, I do not necessarily think they are at risk of cutting their dividends in the near future. However, I do think that for total return-oriented investors, the risk-reward profile is very unattractive right now. This is particularly true given that the risk of a recession has risen in recent months.

As a result, I would steer clear of both of these investments until they offer more of a margin of safety or we get greater clarity on the health of the economy as trade deals get worked out and the U.S. budget and fiscal situation also gain greater clarity. This is why we are steering clear of these opportunities in favor of more defensively positioned and more opportunistically priced high-yield investments at High Yield Investor.

data.title: PAXS: Repositioning According To Its Outlook Might Be Difficult
data.description:
PIMCO Access Income Fund (NYSE:PAXS), a multi-sector fixed-income closed-end fund is back in focus today. We last covered the fund in February, where we disagreed with its interim active outlook, and therefore assigned a 'hold' consensus.

Material time has passed since our latest coverage as numerous events have impacted the credit markets. Therefore, we decided to re-engage and communicate our updated views on PAXS to Seeking Alpha's reader base.

Without further ado, here's what we think about PAXS' latest prospects.

PAXS: A Reminder Of Its Optics & Recent Performance

The chart below shows that PAXS has delivered positive total returns since its inception. Some of those returns were surrendered in early 2025 as U.S. option-adjusted spreads spiked. Moreover, qualitative factors such as a global trade war and talks of fiscal dominance intensified, contributing to uncertainty. PAXS has stabilised ever since, providing a valuable point of analysis.

Let's remind ourselves of PAXS' core strategy: PAXS is an opportunistic fund primarily emphasising income and targeting capital appreciation as a secondary effect. Its fund overview shows that it is relatively unconstrained. PAXS has a notable U.S., high-yield credit, non-agency mortgage, and non-U.S. developed tilt. We'll assess these in combination with the fund's outlook but note that PAXS' exposure is bound to change at various points-in-time.

Here's a quantified view of PAXS' latest reported exposure. Most of it is discussed in the next section. However a few critical points have to be mentioned; they're outlined below the diagram.

  • MV% shows market-weighted presence in a portfolio, whereas DWE% illustrates the duration-weighted exposure. Duration describers interest rate sensitivity.
  • The difference between effective maturity (7.48 years) and funding maturity (3.97 years) is known as a duration gap, illustrating the interest rate sensitivity difference between funding and invested assets.
  • Non-agency mortgage refers to non-GSE guaranteed mortgages.
  • Not shown in the diagram is the visual below, which conveys PAXS' current leverage structure – Reverse repo is a mechanism where a counterparty posts collateral in return for a short-term loan. To our understanding, PAXS is communicating from the counterparty's view; in reality, PAXS engages in a repo transaction (from its perspective) where it receives funds for securities it pledges; kindly see a breakdown of its holdings for details of this funding method.

Market Commentary

PIMCO

PIMCO communicated via its PAXS statements that it maintains a cautious view of the economy. It states that it prefers high-quality idiosyncratic risk in senior parts of capital structures. Moreover, it supports real estate and asset-backed opportunities, deeming them solid. That being said, PAXS' mitigating abilities is limited as its theme is to seek income in higher-yielding sectors. In other words, while management may have identified emerging risks, it can't necessarily neutralise those risks because it needs to maintain return-seeking exposure.

Ours

We disagreed with PIMCO's outlook in our previous analysis. However, in contrast, we agree with its current take on the market for the following reasons:

  • The economy seems soft. Anecdotally, we've seen that many believe abated tariff tensions will re-ignite growth. However, we remain of the option that there will be a long-lasting loss in confidence, potentially leading to dents in financial liberation/globalisation.
  • In addition to trade tensions, we've seen economic data weaken in recent quarters, which is quite natural given that the economy is cyclical and has experienced strong growth in recent years (it has to taper at some stage). The late-cycle effects have run through most developed economies with Japan re-entering flat growth (coupled with monetary struggles), the U.S. entering negative growth, the UK suffering from a weaker labour market, and the EU's throughout-the-cycle growth resuming its underwhelming trajectory – below is a chart showing Fidelity's business cycle summation.
  • From a market-based perspective, we've seen stocks rally year-to-date and high-yield option-adjusted spreads remain low. Moreover, mortgage rates have been resilient, mostly due to elevated U.S. treasury yields.

How do we see all this playing into PAXS' allocation strategy?

Firstly, we support PAXS' view of mortgages as a temporary ballast with outlying risk-return prospects. That said, we prefer U.S. agency mortgages, instead of PAXS' CMBS and non-agency exposure. Furthermore, we think PAXS' intention to isolate idiosyncratic risk in corporate credit is smart but we see systematic risk predominating the corporate credit environment and disregard PAXS' existing high-yield credit exposure. Lastly, we think PAXS' global exposure might provide benefits, especially during a time when U.S. exceptionalism is being challenged. That being said, few credit markets present the depth of the U.S.', therefore we'll likely see PAXS maintain a strong U.S. tilt, despite it attempts at geographic diversification.

In essence, we support PIMCO's outlook but we don't expect PAXS to fully reposition, or at least not soon enough.

Distributions

PAXS' ten-year trailing distribution history shows a blend of income distributions and return of capital. Moreover, it paid a special amount in December 2022, which I highlighted in the table below.

The vehicle has a strong yield, settling at the higher end of 11%. As previously mentioned, most of its distributions derive from income. Some of the distributed capital is from ROC, which isn't directly taxable but reduces an investors capital gains cost basis.

Whether the yield on PAXS makes sense for an investor would be related to personal preference. We don't think chasing yield is prudent at this stage of the credit cycle, especially for a fund like PAXS, which hosts a maximum drawdown of 22.28%, a 5% monthly value-at-risk of 8.08%, and an expected shortfall of 9.23%. These figures might understate PAXS' true risk as we haven't witnessed what PAXS behaves like under extreme market conditions such as the great financial crisis, COVID-19, and the tech bubble.

Valuation Premium (Discount)

There's a dislocation between PAXS' market value at NAV. Its market value stood at $15.05 and NAV at $15.22 when it reported on June 9th. While its NAV usually oscillates around its market value, we don't see a clear-cut arbitrage opportunity, especially considering our directional view of the CEF.

Expenses

PAXS' expenses need to interpreted carefully because its total expense ratio includes interest as an expense. The ex-interest expense ratio currently stands at 2.19% with management fees at 1.25%. We might see lower interest expenses occur in late 2025 or early 2026 as lower Fed target rates seem probably, which could lower repo rates. Despite that, as things stand, PAXS' total expense ratio seems high for the time being.

Conclusion

While we agree with PAXS' updated market outlook, we think its existing exposure remains unattractive amid emerging credit risk. Although PAXS might reposition into more suitable assets, pivoting a large income-seeking portfolio poses challenges.

PAXS existing holdings statement illustrates sustained focus on CMBS and non-agency RMBS. We prefer agency RMBS for the time being. Moreover, PAXS' exposure to high-yield credit remains elevated, which we deem a risk as option-adjusted spreads are flimsy. On the plus side, we like the vehicle's push for geographic diversification and ex-U.S. dollar bets.

With a yield above 11% and a price-to-NAV discount add allure, some might deem PAXS investable. However, we simply think systematic risks are too high and PAXS' quantitative risk metrics spell susceptibility.

In essence, we believe PAXS will have it's day as best-in-class asset but the credit environment is heating up, posing a series of short-term headwinds.

Our Outlook: Hold
